terms.definition: An algorithm that orders elements using only a `<` comparator; bounded below by Ω(n log n) in the worst case.
An algorithm that uses key structure (digits, range) to sort without comparing pairs; can run in O(n + k).
A sort that preserves the relative order of elements with equal keys; needed for clean multi-key sorting.
A sort that uses O(1) (or O(log n)) extra space and rearranges the input itself; heapsort and quicksort qualify.
A sort whose running time improves on inputs with pre-existing order; Timsort and insertion sort are adaptive.
The hybrid mergesort + insertion-sort algorithm used by Python's `sorted` / `list.sort`; stable, adaptive, O(n log n) worst case.
Divide-and-conquer sort that splits in half, recursively sorts, and merges; O(n log n) time and O(n) space; stable.
Pivot-and-partition sort; O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst case; in-place but not stable.
Sort that builds a max-heap then repeatedly pops the root; O(n log n) worst case, in-place, not stable.
Multi-pass non-comparison sort that processes one digit (or character) at a time; O(n · d) for d-digit keys.
Mergesort-style algorithm for data that does not fit in RAM; reads/writes runs from disk and k-way merges them.
